title: God's Will and Discernment
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
source_files: God’s Will and Discernment - 1 Thessalonians 4_3 - Elder Nick Anderson.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Two Wills of God
subpoints: Many Christians make a connection between discernment and the will of God — but which will?
God has two wills: his decreative (secret) will and his preceptive (revealed) will
God's secret will: his eternal decrees — mostly not revealed to us (Deuteronomy 29:29; Isaiah 46:9-11)
God's revealed will: his commands in Scripture — what he tells us to do
We are not trying to discern God's secret will — that is off-limits and impossible

outline.title: What Is God's Revealed Will? Three Summary Principles
subpoints: 1 Thessalonians 4:3: 'This is the will of God — your sanctification.' If you take nothing else from this lesson, take this
First: God wants us to be filled with the Holy Spirit (Ephesians 5:17-18)
Second: God wants us to be sanctified (1 Thessalonians 4:3)
Third: God wants us to be thankful (1 Thessalonians 5:18)
These three areas actually cover all of life — they are God's revealed will for every situation

outline.title: Discerning God's Will in Practice: Three Stages
subpoints: Romans 12:2: 'be transformed by the renewing of your mind, that you may prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God'
Stage 1: Transforming our minds — steeping ourselves in Scripture (metamorphosis)
Stage 2: Applying truth — using biblical principles when the Bible gives no explicit command
Stage 3: Daily life on the fly — the things in our heart are the things that will come out in the moment
Ephesians 5:8-10: 'finding out what is acceptable to the Lord' by walking as children of light

outline.title: Practical Decision-Making: Biblical Principles
subpoints: Where God's commands are explicit — just obey (do not steal, be sober, tithe, submit to your husband)
Where the Bible gives no explicit command — pray, use wisdom, apply biblical principles, and then choose
Example (moving cross-country): Is there a good church? Can you provide for your family? What are your motives?
We discover God's will by knowing and applying his Word, not by seeking inner peace, liver-shivers, or special revelation
Key principle: nine-tenths of doing God's will is being where you're supposed to be, doing what you're supposed to do

outline.title: Conclusion: God's Will as Delight
subpoints: Psalm 40:8: 'I delight to do your will, O my God, and your law is within my heart'
With God's law in our hearts, we can know the truth and delight in obeying God
Discernment is the skill of understanding and applying God's Word with the purpose of separating right from wrong
Stat: 43% of evangelical Christians agreed that Jesus was a great teacher but not God — showing the urgency of knowing the Bible
We live out what we believe by doing what is consistent with God's revealed will

summary:
In this evening lesson from the Discernment series, Elder Nick Anderson addresses the connection between discernment and the will of God. He begins by establishing a fundamental distinction that is often missing from popular discussions: God has two wills — his secret, decreative will (his eternal decrees, which are mostly not revealed) and his revealed, preceptive will (his commands in Scripture). Seeking to know God's secret will — what he has planned for your future — is not the goal of discernment and is, in fact, off-limits.

The heart of the lesson is the argument that God's revealed will is not mysterious or elusive — it is stated plainly in passages like 1 Thessalonians 4:3: 'This is the will of God — your sanctification.' Elder Anderson summarizes God's revealed will under three categories: he wants us to be filled with the Holy Spirit, to be sanctified, and to be thankful. These three categories cover all of life and are the lens through which we make decisions.

The second half of the lesson is practical, focusing on how believers discern God's will in specific situations. Three stages are outlined: (1) mind transformation through ongoing immersion in Scripture, (2) applying truth from Scripture to situations where no explicit command exists, and (3) the daily, in-the-moment choices that flow from what is already in our hearts. Elder Anderson works through two worked examples — should I move cross-country, and should I lose weight — to show how biblical principles (not special revelation, open doors, or feelings of peace) should govern these decisions.

The lesson concludes with Psalm 40:8 as the telos: the goal is not merely to know what God wants but to delight in doing it — to have God's law written on the heart so that discernment becomes natural rather than labored. A striking statistic — that 43% of self-identified evangelical Christians deny the deity of Christ — underscores the urgency of deeply knowing Scripture as the foundation for all discernment.
transcript:
## Opening Prayer

Dear Lord, we thank you again for your goodness to us and your mercies. We thank you that we can study your Word tonight. I pray that you would give us grace to learn from you, and I pray that you give me wisdom. We ask in the solemn name of Christ. Amen.

## Introduction: Chapter 6 — God's Will and Discernment

So we're going to continue our study on discernment this evening. We're moving into the lesson on God's will and discernment — the topic that caused me much consternation when I was younger. So hopefully this will be helpful to everyone here tonight.

In lesson three we saw that many — if not most — Christians make a connection between discernment and the will of God. In the past two lessons, Pastor Tito taught us that discernment is primarily concerned with the truth of God. And in this lesson we will focus on how discernment is actually related to the will of God — and one of the primary things we're going to see is how the will of God is founded upon and inseparable from what is true about God, and how that informs our own decisions about what we ought to do in our life.

A book that might be helpful to some people if you want to study this further is Kevin DeYoung's *Just Do Something*. The subtitle is worth buying the book for: *A Liberating Approach to Finding God's Will, or How to Make a Decision Without Dreams, Visions, Fleeces, Impressions, Open Doors, Random Bible Verses, Casting Lots, Liver Shivers, Writing in the Sky, etc.* He was trying to be a Puritan with that subtitle. He almost got there. It's a really good and very helpful book — it's very short.

## God Has Two Wills

So we'll start off by saying that God has two wills. Does that sound strange to anybody?

Okay, so yes, God has two wills. The first one is his *decreative* will, and the second one is his *preceptive* will. Theologians like to use fancy words that people can't understand, so I'll explain.

The first one is God's will of *decree* — also called his *secret will*. The second one is God's will of *command* — also called his *revealed will*.

God's decreed will is his secret will. We don't know what God has decreed — mostly. And his commands — what he tells us to do — that is his revealed will. This is often badly misunderstood in the church. When people often want to know *what is God's will for my life*, they say that without really understanding what they mean by God's will. They don't even know that there are actually two wills in God.

Our catechism speaks to this: *The practical question of how to know the will of God for our lives cannot be solved with any degree of accuracy unless we have some prior understanding of the will of God in general. Without the distinctions we have made, our pursuit of the will of God can plunge us into hopeless confusion and consternation. When we seek the will of God, we must first ask ourselves: which will are we seeking to discover?*

### God's Will of Decree (His Secret Will)

What has God actually decreed to take place in time? Like I said, this is mostly secret. It belongs to God. It's not for us to know.

As we read through the Bible, we see that at times God did reveal to the prophets and to his people things that would happen. But mostly that's not what he does. The prophets mostly did not tell the future — and growing up, I thought that's what they did. But telling the future was a very, very, very small part of what they actually did. Most of what they did was telling people that they needed to stop sinning and repent and do what God told them to do.

So God's decreed will is his secret will — it's mostly not revealed to us. This includes everything that he has ordained, everything that comes to pass, everything that he ordained before the world was, everything that happens in time. That is all part of his decreed will.

The Westminster Shorter Catechism says: *The decrees of God are his eternal purpose, according to the counsel of his will, whereby, for his own glory, he has foreordained whatsoever comes to pass.*

And this is what the prophet Isaiah spoke about. Isaiah 46:9-11:

> *For I am God, and there is no other; I am God, and there is none like me, declaring the end from the beginning, and from ancient times things that are not yet done, saying, "My counsel shall stand, and I will do all my pleasure... Indeed I have spoken it; I will also bring it to pass. I have purposed it; I will also do it."*

So God is saying that he is sovereign and that he rules over all things — and that means all things. Whatsoever comes to pass — as the catechism says. Actions of humans, actions of animals, weather systems, anything you can think of. God is sovereign and he rules over it. He has ordained it to happen.

Yes — that even includes the evil actions of men. The worst act of evil ever committed was ordained by God: it was ordained to happen to his only Son Jesus Christ. Acts 4:27-28:

> *For truly against your holy servant Jesus, whom you anointed, both Herod and Pontius Pilate, with the Gentiles and the people of Israel, were gathered together to do whatever your hand and your purpose determined before to be done.*

Even the crucifixion of Christ was ordained by God — the most evil act in history was actually planned by him.

And like I said, his secret will is hidden from us. He has chosen not to reveal his decrees to us. So when we talk about discerning God's will, we are not talking about trying to figure out the future. We are not trying to figure out his decrees. He has not promised to reveal the future to us, and therefore it's something we ought not pry into.

Remember Deuteronomy 29:29: *The secret things belong to the Lord our God, but the things that are revealed belong to us and to our children forever, that we may do all the words of this law.* We actually see both of God's wills in that one verse: the secret things belong to God, and the things that are revealed belong to us — that we may obey the words of this law.

Spurgeon speaks to this: *If our quest is to penetrate the hidden aspects of his will, then we have embarked on a fool's errand. We are trying the impossible and chasing the untouchable. Such a quest is not only an act of foolishness but an act of presumption. There is a very real sense in which the secret will and the secret counsel of God is none of our business and is off-limits to our speculative investigation.*

In other words, knowing God's secret will is off-limits. So with that, let's talk about his revealed will.

### God's Will of Command (His Revealed Will)

This is everything that we read in the Bible that directs us how we are to live. If you've ever seen the meme where a little character is standing there shouting at the clouds, "God, speak to me!" — and in the next frame there's a giant hand coming down and handing him a Bible — that is how God speaks to us. We read the Bible. Everything in the Bible that God commands us to do is his revealed will for us.

So 1 Thessalonians 4:3 summarizes what God's will is for us. If you don't take anything else from tonight's lesson, take this:

> *This is the will of God — your sanctification.*

Now you know what God's will is for your life. Your sanctification is God's will. God's will for you is to live a holy life, to become more like Christ, to glorify him with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength, to grow in the knowledge of him, to love him more, and to let the love of Christ shine through you.

And the Bible has a lot to say about God's will, and I'm going to talk about three general principles:

First, God wants us to be filled with the Holy Spirit — Ephesians 5:17-18: *Therefore do not be unwise, but understand what the will of the Lord is. And do not be drunk with wine, in which is dissipation, but be filled with the Spirit.*

Secondly, God wants us to be sanctified — and I already mentioned this.

And thirdly, God wants us to be thankful — 1 Thessalonians 5:18: *In everything give thanks, for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus for you.*

These three areas actually cover all of life and display to us what God's revealed will is for us.

## Discerning God's Will: Three Stages

And this is what Paul writes about in Romans 12. Romans 12:2:

> *Do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind, that you may prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God.*

The word *prove* there doesn't mean to verify or argue for it — it means a way of testing, examining, scrutinizing: if what we're doing is right or not. And when we're transformed by the renewing of our minds, we are able to discern what God's will is. This doesn't mean we can discern God's secret will — it simply means we'll know how to live in a way that pleases God and honors him.

Ephesians 5:8-10 also draws a line between knowing the truth and discerning God's will: *Walk as children of light, for the fruit of the Spirit is in all goodness, righteousness, and truth, finding out what is acceptable to the Lord.* By finding out what is acceptable to the Lord, we find out what his will is, and that should be our desire.

So if we are to discover God's will, we need to discern what is pleasing to him. And we do this in two ways: first, by imitating Christ; and second, by knowing our Bible.

A quick pause here: Ligonier Ministries puts out a State of Theology survey each year. One of the things they sadly reported: 43 percent of people who claim to be evangelical, born-again Christians agreed with this statement: *Jesus was a great teacher, but he was not God.* That is up roughly 10-15 percent from the previous year. What that means is there's a ton of work to be done. We have more access to Bibles, to theology, to videos, to resources than ever before — and we are so ignorant of God's Word. If we had 100 people in this room, 43 of them would not believe that Jesus is God — which makes them heretics, by the way. A damnable heresy. So this is why we emphasize knowing the Bible here. Because we have to know what God's Word says.

So a quick summary of the two wills of God: there's his decrees, which are secret, and there are his commands, which are known. We are not trying to pry into the will of God's decrees — we are to study diligently to know what he commands. He wants us to be filled with the Spirit, sanctified, and thankful. And we discover God's will by studying his Word.

But I can already hear the questions: *How do I discern what is God's will for me when I have to make an important decision?* Well, how do we actually decide?

### Principle 1: Where God's Commands Are Explicit — Just Obey

Should I rob this bank? No — God tells us not to steal. Should I get drunk? No — God commands us to be sober. Should I tithe? Yes. Should I submit to my husband? Yes. When we see the Bible commanding us, we don't question whether we should obey — hopefully that's pretty clear.

### Principle 2: Where the Bible Gives No Explicit Guidance

God gives us the freedom and the responsibility to make a decision and choose what we'll do. We pray, we ask God for wisdom, we rely on him, and we choose what is best according to what he has revealed to us.

For example: which church should I attend? There's no explicit command saying *Steve, you must attend this church.* We have to make a decision, and the Bible gives us principles — we're going to talk about how we'd make that kind of decision.

When the Bible doesn't give explicit guidance, we use discernment. Whatever the circumstance we find ourselves in — after we've eliminated the things God says not to do, prayed, used wisdom, and applied biblical principles — then we know we have made a moral and wise choice. And then we just trust God that he's going to work out the details, because we know that all things work together for good for those who love God.

And an important note: we haven't tried to discern what God's secret will is in any of this. We're not seeking revelations from God. We're not looking for a liver-shiver. We're not seeking inner peace about our decision — that's a very popular one: *Well, I have peace about divorcing my wife, so I'm just going to go ahead and do that.* No. And we're not opening to a random page and pointing to a verse and saying, "That's God's will for me." No.

Someone might ask: *How do I know if I'm actually making the right decision?* A wise pastor once told me: nine-tenths of doing God's will is being where you're supposed to be and doing what you're supposed to be doing at that time. So most of the time, if you're where you're supposed to be doing the things you're supposed to be doing, you're good — you're doing God's will.

If we love God and desire to please him — meaning we're not doing the things he's forbidden, we're striving to grow in holiness, we're repenting when we sin, we're ruling out the things he's forbidden — therefore we are in the will of God. And we trust what Proverbs 3:5-6 says: *Trust in the Lord with all your heart, lean not on your own understanding, and in all your ways acknowledge him, and he shall direct your paths.*

Ultimately, we may not know if we've made the best possible decision. There are times when we lay all this out, make a decision — not a sinful decision — and things don't go the way we expected. And yet the Lord uses those things to sanctify us, because he works all things for good.

## Three Stages of Understanding and Obeying God's Will

Let me give you three stages from Romans 12.

**Stage 1: Mind Transformation.** Romans 12:2: *Be transformed by the renewing of your mind.* Our minds undergo a transformation when they are steeped in God's Word. The Greek word is where we get *metamorphosis* — so the caterpillar goes through a metamorphosis and becomes a butterfly. That's kind of what happens with our minds when we're steeped in God's Word. It is completely changed into something different. And this is not a stage we ever graduate from.

**Stage 2: Applying Truth.** The truth we've learned in stage one, we apply to every area of life. Some situations are simple. Some situations are complicated, less clear — and we may struggle, we may pray, we may ask many people for advice, and ultimately we may just have to make a decision and hope we've made the right one.

For example: should I move cross-country? Is there a good church? Will you be able to provide for your family? What are your motives for moving — are you moving strictly for selfish desires? Is there anything that will clearly hinder your spiritual growth? These are the types of things you need to ask from the Scriptures.

Another example: should I lose weight and get fit? The typical answer might be: *Our bodies are the temple of the Holy Spirit, so yes, I should.* And while there's some truth to that, there are probably other questions to ask. How much time will it take? Is it going to take me away from my family for hours every day? What's my motivation — do I want people to admire me? Is being physically fit an idol? [Discussion with congregation about nuancing this — the point is that anything good can become an idol.]

**Stage 3: Daily Life.** We don't have the luxury of praying about every single thing we do in life. We make decisions on the fly, say things quickly, and it's in those day-by-day moments — at work, with family, in the daily grind — when the things in our heart are the things that will come out. And that informs us of where we need sanctification.

## Conclusion: God's Will as Delight

Psalm 40:8: *I delight to do your will, O my God, and your law is within my heart.*

With God's law in our hearts, we can know the truth and delight in obeying God. And then we will be discerning people — godly, discerning people. We use discernment to distinguish between good and evil. We use it to learn what God would have us do in any given situation by looking at biblical truth. We separate out the good options from the bad options. We apply the truth to the situations we find ourselves in, day by day. And we make decisions based on what is true about God and what will bring him glory and honor.

Let's pray.

Gracious God, we thank you that you are good to us. We thank you that we have your Word, and we pray that you would help us learn it — learn it deeply, learn it well. We pray that our minds would be transformed by your Word, and that because of that transformation we would be able to discern what your will is in every situation we find ourselves in. We ask your blessing on the rest of our evening. We pray this in Christ's name. Amen.
